meta.title: The Harbour That Priced Itself
meta.theme: how a single crowded fishing harbour rationed its berths by price

format.format_verified: 2026-07: five sentences, TITA, +3 with no negative marking, consistent in released papers 2016 to 2025; re-verify against each new official paper

traps.description: No engineered decoy. The outlier is right on the topic of prices and scarcity but pitched at a global, economy wide scope, while the core is a tight case study of one harbour.

meta.learning_objective: notice when a sentence widens from a specific case to a claim about the whole economy

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. General microeconomics of congestion pricing; no specific text adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Odd One Out milestone); ODD_MAN_OUT_BIBLE frameworks applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Easy tier, first modest overlap. The core is a single harbour case study; the outlier makes a sweeping claim about prices across a whole economy. Same topic, wrong scope. Outlier at B. Owner review pending.

sentences.label: A
sentences.text: For years the small fishing harbour ran on a first come rule, and by dawn every good berth near the market was already taken.

sentences.label: B
sentences.text: Across an entire economy, prices coordinate millions of separate decisions that no planner could ever gather in one place.

sentences.label: C
sentences.text: So the harbour master tried something new: he auctioned the dozen best berths each morning to whoever bid highest for them.

sentences.label: D
sentences.text: Almost at once the crush at dawn eased, because a skipper now weighed the fee against the day's likely catch before racing in.

sentences.label: E
sentences.text: The berths went, in the end, to the boats that could turn them into the most value, which was exactly what the crush had hidden.

builder.prompt: Build the four sentence argument about the harbour, from the problem to what pricing revealed. The sentence left out is your answer.

explanation.spine_note: The four sentences argue one case from start to finish. A sets the problem, a harbour rationed by racing rather than value. C introduces the fix, a morning auction. D shows the immediate effect, the dawn crush easing. E draws the conclusion, the berths going to the boats that valued them most. Problem, intervention, effect, point.

core_roles.label: A
core_roles.why_here: It states the specific problem the whole paragraph solves: a harbour where first come wasted the best berths.

core_roles.label: C
core_roles.why_here: It supplies the intervention that answers A, the harbour master's auction, and its opening so ties it to the problem.

core_roles.label: D
core_roles.why_here: It reports what the auction changed, and its because clause explains the mechanism, skippers now weighing fee against catch.

core_roles.label: E
core_roles.why_here: It states what the episode demonstrates, the berths reaching the boats that could use them best.

links.explanation: C opens with so, marking itself as the harbour master's response to the exact problem A has just laid out.
The auction introduced in C is D's given, and D adds the new consequence: the dawn crush easing.
E's the berths and the crush point back to the same harbour and the same problem, closing the case D advanced.

explanation.violation_named: Sentence B is right topic at the wrong scope. The core is a tight case study of one harbour and its dozen berths. B makes a sweeping claim about how prices coordinate an entire economy, millions of decisions no planner could gather. The subject, prices and scarcity, matches. The reach does not.
explanation.why_it_breaks: Every core sentence stays inside the harbour: this master, these berths, that dawn. B leaves the harbour entirely for the whole economy and never returns. It is the kind of grand statement a textbook might use to frame the story, but the paragraph the four sentences build is the story itself, not its frame, and there is no join where a claim about millions of decisions can attach to a claim about twelve berths.
explanation.trap_exposed: B shares the core's vocabulary of prices, coordination and scarcity, and it is entirely true, so it feels like the paragraph's thesis stated grandly. That is the pull of a right topic, wrong scope sentence: its truth and its keywords hide the change in altitude. But the four core sentences never rise above one harbour, and a claim about the whole economy simply looks down from too high to join them.

exclusion_analysis.label: A
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It reads like scene setting, so a reader judging sentence by sentence may treat it as skippable background.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without A there is no problem for the auction to solve, and C's so answers a question the paragraph never asked.

exclusion_analysis.label: C
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: It sits in the middle of the causal chain, so before the paragraph is built its exact position is the least obvious.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without C the intervention vanishes: the crush eases in D for no stated reason, and the argument loses its cause.

exclusion_analysis.label: D
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: D and B both explain how prices guide behaviour, so a hurried read holds both as candidates and guesses.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without D the auction has no visible effect, and E's conclusion about value rests on a change the paragraph never showed.

exclusion_analysis.label: E
exclusion_analysis.why_tempting: Its closing phrase, which the crush had hidden, has a reflective register that stands out from the plainer sentences.
exclusion_analysis.what_breaks: Without E the paragraph stops at a practical result and never states its point, that pricing revealed which boats valued the berths.

explanation.detection_habit: Fix the scope of the paragraph before judging any sentence. When four sentences stay inside one case and a fifth speaks for the whole world, the world sized sentence is usually the one that does not belong.

mentor.challenge: One sentence here is the biggest, truest claim of the five. Ask whether this paragraph is that big.
mentor.takeaway: The widest and truest sentence can still be the odd one. Belonging is about reach, not grandeur.
